title: The Hidden Physics of the Transformer

yaml.title: The Hidden Physics of the Transformer

markdown.markdown:
```{=html}
<script>document.body.classList.add('book-page');</script>
```

The most revealing problems in computer science are often not computational problems at all. They are
physical ones.


This observation has appeared repeatedly throughout the history of computing, although it is easy to
overlook because software has a peculiar habit of concealing the machinery beneath it. An algorithm is
written as though memory were infinite, processors instantaneous, and communication free. Reality, of
course, is less accommodating. Every instruction ultimately becomes the movement of electrical signals
through silicon, every data structure occupies a finite location in memory, and every computation
competes with countless others for access to buses, caches and interconnects. The elegance of an
algorithm on paper is therefore only one half of the story. The other half is whether the underlying
machine can execute it efficiently.


Large language models have reached precisely this point in their evolution.


For much of the past decade, discussion surrounding transformer architectures has been dominated by
mathematics. Attention mechanisms, positional encodings, normalization strategies, activation
functions and scaling laws have understandably occupied the spotlight. They are intellectually satisfying
subjects because they describe how a model learns. Yet there is another, quieter discipline that
determines whether those ideas can be deployed at scale: computer architecture.


It is difficult to appreciate this distinction until one considers a simple question.


What, exactly, is a GPU doing while a language model generates the next token?


The intuitive answer is that it is performing matrix multiplications. This is certainly true, but it is
incomplete in much the same way that describing a modern city as "a place where people work" is true
without explaining traffic, electricity, water distribution or public transport. Computation is only one
activity occurring within the machine. An equally significant amount of time is spent retrieving operands
from memory, transferring intermediate results between processing units, synchronizing execution
across thousands of concurrent threads and waiting for data that has not yet arrived.


The larger a model becomes, the more these invisible activities begin to dominate.


This phenomenon is neither unique to artificial intelligence nor particularly new. Computer architects
have spent decades confronting what has become known as the memory wall . Processor performance
has historically improved at a substantially faster rate than memory bandwidth and latency. Arithmetic
units capable of performing trillions of floating-point operations per second are of limited value if they
must repeatedly pause while waiting for the data upon which those operations depend. A processor
deprived of information resembles an assembly line deprived of components: its theoretical
productivity becomes largely irrelevant.


The implications for transformer models are profound.


Consider inference rather than training. Every generated token requires the model to revisit
information accumulated throughout the conversation. Hidden states from previous tokens are
transformed into keys and values and stored within the key-value cache, allowing subsequent attention
operations to reuse earlier computations rather than recompute them from scratch. Conceptually, this
is an elegant optimization. Computationally, it introduces an entirely different problem.


The cache grows monotonically with context length.


A conversation containing a few hundred tokens presents little difficulty. A conversation spanning
hundreds of thousands of tokens does. Every newly generated token now requires reading an
increasingly large collection of keys and values distributed across high-bandwidth memory. The
arithmetic required to compute attention may remain manageable, particularly following innovations
such as FlashAttention, which dramatically improved memory access patterns during the attention
computation itself. Yet before the GPU can perform those optimized computations, it must first retrieve
the relevant information from memory. The cost of transportation begins to rival, and eventually
exceed, the cost of calculation.


The distinction is subtle but fundamental.


Most discussions of transformer efficiency remain preoccupied with reducing the number of floating-
point operations. Increasingly, however, FLOPs are not the scarce resource. Modern tensor cores are
astonishingly efficient at multiplying matrices. What they cannot do is eliminate the physical distance
separating computation from data. Information must still traverse the memory hierarchy, moving from
high-bandwidth memory into on-chip SRAM, through shared memory and registers, before finally
reaching the execution units responsible for computation. Every journey consumes time. Every journey
consumes energy. Every unnecessary journey compounds across billions of tokens.


One might reasonably ask why these issues appear so suddenly. After all, transformer architectures
have existed since 2017. Why did memory bandwidth emerge as the defining bottleneck only recently?


The answer lies in an irony familiar throughout the history of engineering. Success often creates the
conditions under which entirely new limitations become visible.


Early transformer models were sufficiently small that arithmetic dominated execution time. There were
simply too few parameters and too little context for memory movement to become overwhelming.
Simultaneously, GPU vendors were delivering extraordinary improvements in computational
throughput. Tensor cores, mixed-precision arithmetic and increasingly sophisticated compiler
optimizations caused the cost of matrix multiplication to fall far more rapidly than the cost of moving
data. The consequence was predictable, though not immediately obvious. Computation became
progressively cheaper while communication remained stubbornly constrained by the physical realities
of semiconductor manufacturing.


The bottleneck migrated.


A useful analogy can be found in transportation engineering. Imagine a city that responds to growing
traffic congestion by continuously building faster automobiles while leaving its roads unchanged.
Initially, travel times improve. Eventually, however, the limiting factor ceases to be the speed of
individual vehicles and becomes the capacity of the road network itself. At that point, constructing an
even faster engine contributes almost nothing to the average journey. Progress instead requires
redesigning intersections, optimizing traffic flow and reducing unnecessary travel altogether.


The transformer finds itself in a remarkably similar situation.


For years, the community devoted extraordinary effort to building increasingly powerful computational
engines. Larger models, faster tensor cores and more capable accelerators delivered spectacular gains.
Yet these improvements gradually exposed another reality. The dominant cost was no longer
performing computation but supplying computation with the information it required. Intelligence, one
might say, had become inexpensive. Transporting intelligence had not.


This observation represents the first genuine departure from the scaling paradigm described in the
previous chapter. If memory movement rather than arithmetic has become the principal constraint,
then entirely different optimization strategies become attractive. Compressing representations
becomes more valuable than accelerating computation. Locality becomes more valuable than raw
throughput. Numerical precision becomes an architectural decision rather than merely a statistical one.
Entire classes of innovations—many of which initially appear modest when viewed in isolation—
suddenly reveal themselves as responses to a common underlying problem.


This is precisely the context in which several seemingly unrelated developments begin to converge.
FlashAttention reorganizes the order in which attention is computed to minimize unnecessary transfers
between high-bandwidth memory and on-chip memory. Grouped Query Attention reduces the amount
of key-value state that must be stored and retrieved during inference. FP8 arithmetic reduces the
volume of information flowing through the memory hierarchy. Compiler technologies fuse kernels to
eliminate intermediate writes back to memory. Even distributed training strategies increasingly
prioritize overlapping communication with computation, recognizing that idle hardware often reflects
unavailable data rather than insufficient arithmetic capability.


None of these techniques attempts to make transformers more intelligent.


They attempt to make transformers less wasteful.


Viewed independently, each innovation appears incremental. Viewed collectively, they reveal something
more significant. The center of innovation had shifted away from inventing fundamentally new learning
algorithms toward understanding where the machine itself was leaking efficiency. Modern AI research
was beginning to resemble high-performance computing, a discipline in which success is rarely
achieved through a single dramatic breakthrough and far more often through relentless elimination of
hidden overhead.


The consequences of this change extend beyond hardware utilization. Once engineers begin viewing
computation as a scarce economic resource rather than an abundant one, every layer of the
transformer becomes open to reconsideration. Why should every parameter participate in generating
every token? Why should every representation remain stored at full precision? Why should every
communication operation block useful work elsewhere in the system? Questions that once seemed
peripheral suddenly become central.


It is here that one encounters perhaps the most consequential architectural idea of the modern transformer era. Surprisingly, it is also one of the oldest.


Long before anyone imagined trillion-parameter language models, researchers had proposed a
deceptively simple idea: perhaps intelligence does not require every part of a network to think all of the
time.


The idea was called **Mixture of Experts** .

For nearly three decades it remained an elegant concept waiting for the surrounding ecosystem to
catch up.
